chunks.page_summary: This document outlines the procedure for integrating Rheonics inline viscometers and density sensors via their Smart Module Electronics unit (SME) with a Siemens S7-400 PLC over PROFIBUS using a Moxa MGate 5111 converter and TIA Portal.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Profibus integration of Rheonics devices using TIA portal

Modified on: Tue, 26 Nov, 2024 at 10:36 PM

What products are involved?
Rheonics SRV viscometer (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/), SRD density-viscosity meter (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/), DVP gas density-viscosity meter (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/), and DVM HPHT density-viscosity meter (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dvm/).

What is the purpose of this article?
This article describes the procedure to establish communication between a Rheonics SME (Modbus TCP) and a S7-400 with a PROFIBUS Interface through a Moxa converter (MGate 5111)

Table of contents

*   1. Overview
*   2. Communication Between SME and a PROFIBUS Interface
*   3. Moxa MGate 5111 Configuration
    *   3.1 Network Settings
    *   3.2 Protocol Conversion Settings
    *   3.3 Modbus TCP Settings
    *   3.4 Profibus Settings
    *   3.5 I/O Data Mapping
*   4. S7-400 Configuration: TIA Portal
    *   4.1 Creating a New Project
    *   4.2 Importing a GSD File
    *   4.3 Adding the Moxa to the Diagram
    *   4.4 Profibus Configuration and Mapping
*   5. Rheonics Digital Signals
*   6. References

1. Overview

Rheonics viscosity and density meters bring fluid intelligence and process control to a broad range of customer applications. Each sensor is equipped with a Smart Module Electronics unit (SME) (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/), which drives the sensor, evaluates its response, and enables communication through all major communication protocols.
Rheonics sensors natively support industrial protocols such as Modbus, Ethernet/IP, Profinet, and HART. Other protocols, like Profibus, are supported through converters. These converters are also available from Rheonics pre-configured to work out-of-the-box with Rheonics sensors. The guide provides comprehensive step-by-step instructions for both configuring the converters for use with Rheonics sensors as well as using
key_facts: The document provides a guide for Profibus integration of Rheonics devices using the TIA portal.
The integration establishes communication between a Rheonics SME (Modbus TCP) and a Siemens S7-400 PLC with a PROFIBUS Interface.
A Moxa MGate 5111 converter is utilized to facilitate communication between Modbus TCP and PROFIBUS.
Rheonics offers SRV viscometer, SRD density-viscosity meter, DVP gas density-viscosity meter, and DVM HPHT density-viscosity meter products.
Each Rheonics sensor is equipped with a Smart Module Electronics unit (SME) that drives the sensor, evaluates its response, and enables communication.
Rheonics sensors natively support industrial protocols including Modbus, Ethernet/IP, Profinet, and HART.
Profibus and other protocols are supported by Rheonics sensors through converters, which can be provided pre-configured by Rheonics.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes a communication solution using a Moxa MGate 5111 gateway to bridge PROFIBUS and Modbus TCP protocols, enabling Rheonics SME instruments to communicate with an S7-400 PROFIBUS Master.
chunks.extracted_text:
pre-configured converters from Rheonics. A comprehensive list of digital and analog communication options available on Rheonics automation instruments can be found here (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/).

Figure 1. Moxa MGate 5111. Image sourced from Moxa's website.

2. Communication Between SME and a PROFIBUS Interface
Customers often find themselves needing to connect devices that use different communication protocols, particularly when expanding or modernizing their systems. A common scenario occurs when an existing network running on PROFIBUS needs to integrate newer Modbus TCP devices, such as smart sensors, HMIs, or third-party control systems. Rather than going through a costly complete system overhaul, a protocol gateway provides a practical solution to bridge these networks.

The MGate 5111 gateways convert data from Modbus RTU/ASCII/TCP, EtherNet/IP, or PROFINET to PROFIBUS protocols. It provides a web console for easy configuration through a web browser using the HTTP/HTTPS protocol. It will be used to establish communication between a Rheonics SME, acting as a Modbus TCP Server, and an S7-400 PROFIBUS interface Figure 2. MGate 5111 Application Example

PROFIBUS Cable
Ethernet Cable
key_facts: Rheonics offers pre-configured converters and a comprehensive list of digital and analog communication options for its automation instruments.
The Moxa MGate 5111 is a protocol gateway used to bridge different communication networks, such as PROFIBUS and Modbus TCP.
The MGate 5111 gateways convert data from Modbus RTU/ASCII/TCP, EtherNet/IP, or PROFINET protocols to PROFIBUS protocols.
Configuration of the MGate 5111 is performed via a web console using HTTP/HTTPS protocol.
The MGate 5111 facilitates communication between a Rheonics SME (acting as a Modbus TCP Server) and an S7-400 PROFIBUS interface.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for configuring the Moxa MGate 5111 device, including initial login credentials and network settings, to enable Ethernet communication with the SME.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 2. MGate 5111 Application Example

The SME network configuration must be set up to enable Ethernet communication between SME and MGate 5111. Detailed instructions for configuring the network are available at
https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-verifying-the-network-setup-on-the-pc-where-the-rcp-software-is-installed
(https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000397449-verifying-the-network-setup-on-the-pc-where-the-rcp-software-is-installed)

3. Moxa MGate 5111 Configuration

Log in to the Moxa device using its default IP address: 192.168.127.254 in any web browser. The default username is admin, and the password is moxa.

[Image: Login screen with 'Account', 'Password' fields and 'Login' button]

Figure 3. MGate 5111 Login

3.1 Network Settings

Network Settings is where the IP Configuration, IP Address, Netmask, Default Gateway, and DNS can be modified. Make sure to enter the appropriate IP Address and Subnet Mask. It should be on the same subnet as the SME (same Ethernet network). An incorrect network configuration may prevent the device from communicating with others on the network.

[Image: Moxa MGate 5111 Network Settings interface]

Figure 4. MGate 5111 Network Settings
key_facts: The SME network configuration must be set up to enable Ethernet communication between SME and MGate 5111.
Detailed instructions for configuring the network are available on the Rheonics support website.
To log in to the Moxa device, use its default IP address 192.168.127.254 in a web browser.
The default username for the Moxa device is 'admin' and the default password is 'moxa'.
The 'Network Settings' section allows modification of IP Configuration, IP Address, Netmask, Default Gateway, and DNS.
The IP Address and Subnet Mask should be on the same subnet as the SME (same Ethernet network).
warnings_notes: An incorrect network configuration may prevent the device from communicating with others on the network.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions and settings for configuring Modbus TCP and PROFIBUS protocol conversion using an MGate 5111 device, including details on Modbus TCP client settings and register mapping for Rheonics sensors.
chunks.extracted_text:
3.2 Protocol Conversion Settings
The MGate 5111 will act as a Modbus TCP Client when connected to Rheonics Modbus TCP Server, and as a PROFIBUS Slave when connected to the S7-400 (PROFIBUS). To configure the roles, on the Protocol Conversion screen select the correct protocols as shown in Figure 5. For this example, Rheonics SME is the Modbus TCP Server and the S7-400 the PROFIBUS Master.

Figure 5. MGate 5111 Protocol Conversion Settings

3.3 Modbus TCP Settings
On the Modbus TCP Settings screen, users can create Modbus commands or requests for the Modbus Server (Rheonics SME). Comprehensive Modbus TCP documentation, including details on registers, data types, response types, testing with Modbus Poll, and troubleshooting, is available on the Rheonics Support Portal: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062 (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062)

Figure 6. Modbus Commands

Table 1 is extracted from https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers); these registers will be requested to the SME and mapped to PROFIBUS so the S7-400 can use them. Modbus commands for Viscosity, Density, Temperature, and Sensor Status input registers will be created for this example.

Address (Dec) | Register (Dec) | Length (Reg.) | Type | Description
--- | --- | --- | --- | ---
Parameter 0 Viscosity Median
40 | 300041 | 2 | Float32 | Parameter 0 value as float
Parameter 1 Density Median
key_facts: The MGate 5111 functions as a Modbus TCP Client when connected to a Rheonics Modbus TCP Server.
The MGate 5111 functions as a PROFIBUS Slave when connected to an S7-400 (PROFIBUS).
For the example configuration, Rheonics SME is designated as the Modbus TCP Server.
For the example configuration, the S7-400 is designated as the PROFIBUS Master.
Comprehensive Modbus TCP documentation is available on the Rheonics Support Portal.
Table 1's data is sourced from a Rheonics support article detailing Modbus TCP input registers.
Modbus commands will be created for Viscosity, Density, Temperature, and Sensor Status input registers for the example configuration.
The requested registers from SME will be mapped to PROFIBUS for use by the S7-400.
diagram_labels: Role2 of MGate 5111 : Modbus TCP Client

Figure 8. Modbus Commands Example

3.4 Profibus Settings

An ID for the PROFIBUS slave needs to be configured. For addresses between 0 and 99, the rotary switch is adjusted to the desired ID number. For addresses above 99, the rotary switch must be set to 99, and the ID is then configured through the web console.
Figure 9 shows the MGate 5111 rotary switches adjusted to an address of 5, where one dial represents the units (5) and the other represents the tens (0).
key_facts: The page illustrates Modbus TCP settings for a client, including initial delay, maximum retries, and response timeout.

Modbus commands are configured for reading Viscosity, Density, Temperature, and Sensor Status from a slave device at IP address 192.168.3.10:502 with Slave ID 255, using Function 4 (Read Input Registers).
Each Modbus command is set to 'Cyclic' trigger with a 'Poll Interval' of 1000 ms.
PROFIBUS slave ID configuration for addresses between 0 and 99 is done via a rotary switch.
For PROFIBUS slave addresses above 99, the rotary switch must be set to 99, and the ID is configured via the web console.
Rotary switches for PROFIBUS ID utilize one dial for units and another for tens, demonstrated with an example of address 5 (units 5, tens 0).
diagram_labels: Role 2 of MGate 5111: Modbus TCP Client

chunks.page_summary: This page describes the configuration of PROFIBUS I/O modules, detailing how to add modules, set data types and lengths, and highlights a critical 244-byte memory limitation on the MGate 5111 device.
extracted_text: Figure 10 also shows an empty I/O Modules table, where new I/O modules can be added by clicking the "Add" button. After clicking the button, the window shown in Figure 11 will appear. The exact data type and length of the Modbus Registers mapped in the previous step need to be specified.

The total size of all PROFIBUS modules is subject to a 244-byte memory limit, as indicated by the error message in Figure 12. This is an important limitation; for some applications, this capacity may be insufficient, and an additional gateway might be necessary.

This 244-byte memory limitation can be insufficient for some applications, potentially requiring an additional gateway.
PROFIBUS modules are used to map Modbus registers for Viscosity, Density, Temperature, and Sensor Status.

3.5 I/O Data Mapping
The gateway's memory allocation can be verified by navigating to the I/O Data Mapping page. The MGate should automatically map the data on both sides of its internal memory. It is necessary that all Modbus commands are correctly mapped to the PROFIBUS modules and that the data sizes match.

I/O Data Mapping
Data flow direction PROFIBUS Master <--> Modbus TCP Server

Mapping address arrangement Automatic

read

M_Viscosity Input 0x51 0 .. 3 4 bytes
M_Density Input 0x51 4 .. 7 4 bytes
M_Temperature Input 0x51 8 .. 11 4 bytes
M_SensorStatus Input 0x10 12 .. 12 1 bytes

read
Role 2 of MGate 5111 : Modbus TCP Client
Your device : Modbus TCP Server
Name Function Internal Address Quantity
Viscosity 4 0 .. 3 4 bytes
Density 4 4 .. 7 4 bytes
Temperature 4 8 .. 11 4 bytes
Sensor Status 4 12 .. 13 2 bytes

Submit

Figure 14. IO Data Mapping

4. S7-400 Configuration: TIA Portal
4.1 Creating a New Project
In TIA Portal, a new project is created and a new device, the S7-400, is added. The exact article number must be selected to avoid any potential errors.

chunks.page_summary: This page describes the process of integrating a Moxa MGate 5111 device into a PROFIBUS network within the Siemens TIA Portal environment by creating a new project and importing a General Station Description (GSD) file.
chunks.extracted_text:
Totally Integrated Automation PORTAL
Figure 15. New Project Creation

4.2 Importing a GSD File

A GSD file is a standardized text file that describes the communication parameters of a PROFIBUS or PROFINET device. For the Moxa MGate 5111, the GSD file includes details such as device identification, supported baud rates, modes (like Freeze and Sync), diagnostic data length, and I/O module configurations. This file is required to integrate and configure the MGate 5111 into a PROFIBUS network using TIA Portal. It can be downloaded from Moxa's website.

The GSD file is imported by selecting Options and then Manage General Station Description Files (GSD), allowing the MGate 5111 to be added to TIA Portal catalog.

Totally Integrated Automation PORTAL
Figure 16. Adding a GSD File

The folder containing the GSD file must be selected, and TIA Portal will install it into the project.

specifications.CPU 416-3 DP_interfaces: 1 x DP/MPI, 1 x DP and 1 slot for IF modules

key_facts: A GSD file is a standardized text file that describes the communication parameters of a PROFIBUS or PROFINET device.
For the Moxa MGate 5111, the GSD file includes device identification, supported baud rates, communication modes (like Freeze and Sync), diagnostic data length, and I/O module configurations.
The GSD file is required to integrate and configure the MGate 5111 into a PROFIBUS network using TIA Portal.
The GSD file for the Moxa MGate 5111 can be downloaded from Moxa's website.
The GSD file is imported in TIA Portal by selecting 'Options' and then 'Manage General Station Description Files (GSD)'.
Importing the GSD file allows the MGate 5111 to be added to the TIA Portal catalog.
The folder containing the GSD file must be selected for TIA Portal to install it into the project.
diagram_labels: Work memory 8MB code and 8MB data, 96KB for PROFIBUS operations with up to 96 connections; 1 x DP/MPI, 1 x DP and 1 slot for IF modules; capable of sending and receiving in cyclic data mode with constant cycle time and processing in isochronous mode, routing, record routing, firmware V7.0

4.4 Profibus Configuration and Mapping
On the Network View, a new PROFIBUS network is created by dragging one port to another. The Moxa PROFIBUS ID that was set on the rotary switches/browser is configured in the parameters section (See Figure 20).

A Siemens PLC, specifically CPU 416-3DP, and a Moxa MGate 5111 slave device are depicted in the network view.
A new PROFIBUS network is established by dragging connections between ports in the Network View.
The PROFIBUS ID for the Moxa device, which can be set via rotary switches or a browser, is configured in a parameters section.
The project named 's7_400_profibus' was created and successfully saved on October 28, 2024, as indicated by the log messages.

Figure 20. Mgate 5111 PROFIBUS Address

The PROFIBUS modules must be added as inputs in the project. Importing the GSD file adds the Moxa device to the TIA Portal catalog and provides information about available module types and sizes. However, the actual modules need to be manually added to the appropriate device slots. Figure 21 shows all the inputs that were imported to TIA Portal along with the MGate 5111.
key_facts: PROFIBUS modules must be added as inputs in the project within TIA Portal.
Importing a GSD file adds the Moxa device (MGate 5111) to the TIA Portal catalog.
The GSD file provides information about available module types and sizes for the Moxa device.
Actual PROFIBUS modules need to be manually added to the appropriate device slots after importing the GSD file.
The Moxa MGate 5111 is configured with a PROFIBUS address of 5 on the PROFIBUS_1 subnet.
The PROFIBUS network connecting the PLC_1 (CPU 416-3DP) and Slave_1 (Moxa MGate 5111) operates at a transmission speed of 1.5 Mbps.
Figure 21 (not shown on this page) illustrates all inputs that were imported into TIA Portal along with the MGate 5111.

The user can double-click or drag each input to add it to a slot. It’s important to verify that the type and size of each module match with the PROFIBUS mapping configured on the MGate 5111. This step is critical, as any mismatch between the inputs and the PROFIBUS modules might prevent communication.

It is important to verify that the type and size of each module match with the PROFIBUS mapping configured on the MGate 5111.
warnings_notes: This step is critical, as any mismatch between the inputs and the PROFIBUS modules might prevent communication.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides instructions for configuring a Moxa MGate 5111 PROFIBUS module within TIA Portal to create new tags for process variables like viscosity, density, and temperature, and to monitor these tags in an S7-400 control logic.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 22. MGate 5111 Slots
To use the data, we need to create new tags in TIA Portal. Select the address where the PROFIBUS module was stored (see Figure 22). Ensure that the correct data type is selected: choose "Real" for 2-word registers and "Word" for 1-word registers.

Figure 23. New Tags Creation on TIA Portal
The user can go online and set the PLC to "Run" mode. If everything is configured correctly, clicking on "Monitor All" should display data in each of the tags. These tags can then be used in the S7-400 control logic.
key_facts: New tags must be created in TIA Portal to use data from the PROFIBUS module.
The data type "Real" should be selected for 2-word registers.
The data type "Word" should be selected for 1-word registers.
The PLC must be set to "Run" mode to monitor data.
If configured correctly, clicking "Monitor All" will display data in the created tags.
The created tags can be integrated into the S7-400 control logic.

chunks.page_summary: This page discusses the advantages of digital communication protocols for Rheonics viscosity and density meters over traditional analog signals, provides a screenshot of a TIA Portal configuration showing monitored values, and lists relevant technical references.
chunks.extracted_text:
Figure 24. Going Online on TIA Portal

5. Rheonics Digital Signals
Digital communication protocols like PROFIBUS have significantly improved industrial communication by addressing the limitations of traditional analog signals. While analog communication, such as 4-20 mA current loops, has been a longstanding standard due to its simplicity and low cost for basic measurements, it falls short in several critical areas.
*   Multiple Data Transmission: Unlike analog signals that transmit only one piece of information per channel, digital protocols allow multiple data types to be sent over a single interface simultaneously.
*   Resistance to Electrical Interference: Digital signals are inherently less susceptible to electrical noise, making them ideal for industrial environments with high electromagnetic interference.
*   Scalability and Flexibility: Digital networks enable multiple devices to communicate over a single network cable or wireless connection. This scalability reduces wiring complexity and facilitates system expansion.
The choice between analog and digital communication ultimately depends on the application. However, for precise and dependable viscosity control, the use of digital communication is highly recommended. For detailed information on all available communication options for Rheonics viscosity and density meters, visit Communication: Rheonics Support.

6. References
1.  Rheonics, "SRV Inline Viscometer." [Online]. Available: https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/ (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-viscometer-srv/)
2.  Rheonics, "SRD Inline Density Meter." [Online]. Available: https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/ (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-density-meter-srd/)
3.  Rheonics, "Electronics and Communication." [Online]. Available: https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/ (https://rheonics.com/electronics-and-communication/)
4.  Rheonics, "Modbus TCP Input Registers." [Online]. Available: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers)
5.  Moxa, MGate 5111 Series User Manual, Version 1.2, September 2023. [Online]. Available: https://www.moxa.com/products (https://www.moxa.com/products)
key_facts: Digital communication protocols like PROFIBUS have significantly improved industrial communication by addressing the limitations of traditional analog signals.
Analog communication (e.g., 4-20 mA current loops) falls short in areas such as multiple data transmission, resistance to electrical interference, and scalability/flexibility.
Digital protocols allow multiple data types to be sent over a single interface simultaneously, unlike analog signals.
Digital signals are inherently less susceptible to electrical noise, making them suitable for industrial environments with high electromagnetic interference.
Digital networks enable multiple devices to communicate over a single network cable or wireless connection, reducing wiring complexity and facilitating system expansion.
For precise and dependable viscosity control, the use of digital communication is highly recommended.
